title: What is an error in Go
instruction:
Most languages throw exceptions — they're like fire alarms that scream until someone handles them.

Go is different. Functions return errors as regular values — like a librarian handing you a book *and* a note: "Here's your book, and here's what went wrong (if anything)."

Run this. The second call returns an error because there is no book with ID 0.
successMessage: nil means "nothing went wrong." A non-nil error means "here's the problem." Two return values, always. That's Go's error philosophy.

title: Checking an error — if err != nil
hint: if err != nil { fmt.Println("Invalid member ID:", err); return }  fmt.Println("Member #:", n)
expectedOutput: Invalid member ID: strconv.Atoi: parsing "abc": invalid syntax

codeChecks.message: Check the error: if err != nil { ... }
Print "Invalid member ID:" when err != nil.
Print "Member #:" and n when there is no error.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: if err != nil — you will type this hundreds of times in your Go career. It becomes muscle memory. And it means you never accidentally ignore a failure.

title: errors.New() — create your own error
instruction:
You can create your own error values using `errors.New()`:

`return 0, errors.New("book not found")`

The `errors` package is part of the standard library.

Add an error case to `findBook`: if `id < 1`, return `""` and a new error `"book not found"`. Otherwise return the book title and `nil`.
hint: import "errors"  then: if id < 1 { return "", errors.New("book not found") }  return "The Go Programming Language", nil

codeChecks.message: Use errors.New() to create the error.
The error message should be "book not found".
Return the book title and nil when the id is valid.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Your own error, your own message. errors.New is the simplest way to create an error. Clean, readable, no setup required.

title: fmt.Errorf() — errors with context
instruction:
`fmt.Errorf` creates an error with a formatted message — like `fmt.Sprintf` but returns an `error`.

`return fmt.Errorf("book %q is not in our catalog", title)`

This lets you include the actual bad value in the error message, which makes debugging much easier.

Add the error case: if `year < 1900` or `year > 2026`, return a formatted error. Otherwise return `nil`.
hint: if year < 1900 || year > 2026 { return fmt.Errorf("invalid year %d: must be between 1900 and 2026", year) }  return nil
expectedOutput: invalid year 1800: must be between 1900 and 2026

codeChecks.message: Use fmt.Errorf() to create the error with the year value.
Include the year value in the error message using %d.
Return nil when the year is valid.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: "invalid year 1800" — the bad value is right there in the error. This is how professional Go code reports errors. Always include context.

title: Ignoring errors — the dangerous shortcut
instruction:
You can ignore the error return value with `_`. Go will not stop you.

```
title, _ := findBook(id)  // silently ignores any error
```

This is the most common source of subtle bugs in Go. If the lookup fails, `title` will be `""` — and you will never know why.

Run this. Both IDs are processed — but the broken one silently produces an empty string. Then fix it by printing a message when the error is not nil.
hint: Change title, _ := to title, err :=  then add: if err != nil { fmt.Println("skipping missing book:", id); continue }

codeChecks.message: Check the error instead of ignoring it with _.
Print a message when a missing book is found.

successMessage: Catalog has [Book 101 Book 102] — correctly skipping the invalid ID with a visible message. Never ignore errors silently. Always handle or at least log them.

title: Multiple error checks in one function
instruction:
A function can have multiple failure points. Check each one and return the first error you find.

`validateBook` below checks three things: title, ISBN, author. Fill in the missing checks.
hint: if title == "" { return errors.New("title cannot be empty") }  if len(isbn) != 13 { return fmt.Errorf("ISBN %s must be exactly 13 characters", isbn) }
expectedOutput: Invalid: ISBN short must be exactly 13 characters

codeChecks.message: Check if title is empty.
Check if ISBN length is not 13.
Replace the TODO comments with your checks.

successMessage: Four tests, four different outcomes. Each check returns early on the first failure. This is the standard Go validation pattern — return the first error found.

title: Custom error types
hint: return LibraryError{Code: "OVERDUE", Message: "book is overdue by 5 days"}

codeChecks.message: Return a LibraryError with Code: "OVERDUE".
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Structured errors with fields you can read programmatically. Not just a message — a type. Next step: check which type of error you got.

title: errors.Is() — identify a specific error
instruction:
When a function can return different errors, you sometimes need to know which one you got.

`errors.Is(err, ErrBookNotFound)` checks if `err` matches a specific sentinel error.

A sentinel error is a package-level variable: `var ErrBookNotFound = errors.New("book not found")`

Run this. Then add a check for `ErrBookOverdue` and print a different message for it.
hint: } else if errors.Is(err, ErrBookOverdue) { fmt.Println("This book is overdue — please return it") }
expectedOutput: This book is overdue — please return it

codeChecks.message: Use errors.Is(err, ErrBookOverdue) to check for the overdue error.
Print "This book is overdue — please return it" for the overdue case.
Replace the TODO comment with your check.

successMessage: Three IDs, three outcomes, zero crashes. errors.Is lets you handle each failure case differently without string comparison. This is the right way to check for specific errors.

title: errors.As() — extract the error type
instruction:
`errors.As` checks if an error is a specific type and extracts it so you can read its fields.

```
var le LibraryError
if errors.As(err, &le) {
    fmt.Println("Error code:", le.Code)
}
```

Run this. The error message alone tells you something went wrong. `errors.As` tells you exactly which code and message.
expectedOutput: Error: [INVALID_SHELF] shelf 200 is out of range
Details: shelf 200 is out of range
successMessage: errors.As extracted the full LibraryError — error code, message, everything. This is how you build rich error handling that does more than just print a string.

title: Wrapping errors — add context without losing the original
instruction:
When your function calls another function that fails, wrap the error to add context:

`return fmt.Errorf("processBook: %w", err)`

The `%w` verb wraps the original error. The caller gets the full context. `errors.Is` and `errors.As` still work through the wrap.

Run this and see how the error grows as it travels up the call stack.
successMessage: The error message shows the full journey: handleBorrowRequest → findBookByID(42) → catalog database unavailable. errors.Is still finds ErrDatabase through all the wrapping. Context without losing the original.

title: panic and recover
instruction:
`panic` stops normal execution immediately. Use it only for truly unrecoverable situations — programming bugs, impossible states.

`recover` catches a panic inside a deferred function. It is Go's equivalent of a last-chance safety net.

Run this. The `safeReturn` function uses `defer` + `recover` to catch the panic and return an empty string instead of crashing.
expectedOutput: Recovered from panic: invalid book ID passed to system
successMessage: The program did not crash. recover caught the panic, printed the message, and returned a fallback value. Use panic+recover sparingly — for most errors, return an error value instead.

title: Fix the broken error check
instruction:
This program is supposed to look up a book by ID and show its title. But it ignores the error — so when the ID is invalid it silently shows an empty string and nobody knows why.

Fix it: check the error from `findBook`. If there is an error, print `"Lookup failed:"` and the error, then return. Otherwise print `"Title:"` and the result.
hint: Change title, _ := findBook(id) to title, err := findBook(id) then add if err != nil { ... }
expectedOutput: Lookup failed: book not in catalog

codeChecks.message: Capture the error: title, err := findBook(id)
Check the error: if err != nil { ... }
Print "Lookup failed:" when the error is not nil.

successMessage: A proper title for valid input, a clear error message for invalid. No silent failures. This is the difference between code that is easy to debug and code that drives you crazy at 2am.

title: Build a library validator
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a library item validator with a custom error type:

1. Define `CatalogError` with `Field` and `Message` string fields and an `Error() string` method
2. Write `validateItem(title string, isbn string, copies int) error` that checks:
   - `title` is not empty → `CatalogError{Field: "title", Message: "cannot be empty"}`
   - `isbn` has exactly 13 characters → `CatalogError{Field: "isbn", Message: "must be 13 characters"}`
   - `copies` is between 1 and 10 → `CatalogError{Field: "copies", Message: "must be between 1 and 10"}`
3. Test with four items — one valid, three each with a different broken field

Expected output:
```
title: cannot be empty
isbn: must be 13 characters
copies: must be between 1 and 10
Go Programming is valid
```
hint: Use len(isbn) != 13 to check the ISBN length.

codeChecks.message: Define a CatalogError struct.
Add Error() string method to CatalogError.
Define the validateItem function.
Check that title is not empty.
Check that ISBN is exactly 13 characters.
Check that copies is between 1 and 10.

successMessage: A real library catalog validator with structured errors. You built it from scratch — custom type, method, three checks, four tests. This exact pattern runs inside production library systems every day.
